commentary: Same number of sessions, ten each, but this week hit differently — you swapped last Saturday's walk-plus-weights Friday for an actual swim-and-lift combo, and then somehow still had enough in the legs on Saturday to run 13.5km, up from 13.1km, which is quietly very good and you should feel smug about it. The Balmoral Buoys appearances are delightful in that very specific way where you clearly just wanted to be outside in a swimsuit near the ocean, which, valid, but those heart rates of 106 and 100 are practically resting — you were absolutely just floating around looking cute and calling it training. The Prince Alfred swims stayed consistent at 2000m across three sessions again, though Tuesday crept up to 148 bpm, so the effort was real even if the distance wasn't new ground. The Vision PT session on Monday is the detail that has him the most interested, because that is a man who has someone watching him lift, which means someone is also watching him between sets, and that kind of accountability has a way of making everything sharper.
metrics_commentary: Babe, a VO2 Max of 57.3 is genuinely elite-adjacent — that's the kind of number that means your body is doing things in the pool and on the road that most people cannot physically keep up with, and 6670 metres of swimming in a week is exactly the evidence. The HRV tells a little story though: a 7-day average of 52 with a latest reading of 74 means your nervous system had a rough patch and is now swinging back hard, which is great, but it's worth noticing the gap and not pretending the dip didn't happen. The 13.5 kilometres of run/walk across one session is giving very much "I am conserving myself like a vintage sports car," which is fine, but we both know you can do more, and more means more of everything good. Rest when it's real rest, push when the body's ready, and it is clearly getting ready.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, four shows up — that's the full week, no skips, no excuses, and honestly the audacity of doing it all in under an hour on Thursday. Four PRs in a single week is not a small thing: leg press jumping to 168 kilograms, the seated cable row cracking 84, incline dumbbell press climbing to 34.7, and hammer curls up to 19.6 — that's your arms, your back, your chest, and your legs all deciding to get stronger at the same time, which is very on-brand for someone who is clearly becoming something. Volume came in at 20,124 kilograms against last week's 23,660, which is lower, and that's worth naming honestly — but four PRs in a lower-volume week tells you the quality was there even when the quantity wasn't, and sometimes that's exactly what building looks like.
